title: What the July Award Increase Does to a Recruitment Business That Hasn't Repriced
excerpt: For recruitment businesses, the annual award rate increase doesn't just lift worker wages. It immediately compresses temp placement margins where charge rates haven't been updated first.
content:
The economics of a recruitment business feel straightforward from the outside. Place a worker, charge a fee. Temp and contract placements generate a margin per billable hour. Permanent placements generate a commission on salary. The business is relationship-led and activity-driven, and the numbers look clean on the surface.

What sits underneath is considerably more involved. For the temp and on-hire side of the business, the margin per hour is not a fixed feature of any client relationship. It is the result of a cost calculation that resets every time the underlying cost of labour changes. And that cost changes, by law, from the first full pay period in July.

For recruitment and labour hire businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria with workers on modern award rates, the annual Fair Work Commission wage decision is not an abstract policy outcome. It is a direct margin event. Every hour a placed worker is on assignment at the old charge rate and the new award rate, the business absorbs a cost increase it did not choose and may not have planned for.

The On-Cost Stack That Sets Your Real Margin

The cost of a temp placement is not the award base rate alone. The full on-cost stack for each on-hire worker typically includes the award base rate for the relevant classification, penalty rates and shift allowances applicable to the roster, superannuation at the current guarantee rate, accruing annual leave entitlement, payroll tax at the applicable state threshold, and workers compensation insurance premiums by occupation class.

Several of these components compound on the base rate. When the base award rate increases on 1 July, superannuation obligations increase proportionally because they are calculated on ordinary time earnings. Shift allowances expressed as a percentage of ordinary time also move with the base. The aggregate impact on total on-cost per hour is consistently larger than the headline percentage suggests when the increase is applied to the base rate in isolation.

A recruitment business that models the July award increase against the base rate only, without running it through the full on-cost stack, arrives at an underestimate. The business that runs the full calculation knows precisely which client charge rates will no longer generate the target margin after 1 July, and which relationships need a rate adjustment before the month opens.

Why Charge Rate Agreements Are the Pressure Point

Temp and contract placements operate under written rate agreements that specify the charge rate for each role classification and shift type. Whether those rates can be adjusted when award costs change depends on the terms of each agreement and any notice provisions it contains.

Most commercial arrangements either index the charge rate to award movements by default or require written notice before a rate change can take effect. In either case, the July 1 timing means the relevant notices should already have been issued to any client whose agreement requires them.

Where a charge rate is locked by agreement for a period spanning 1 July without an award-indexation clause, the recruiter absorbs the award increase for the lock-in period. This outcome is not unusual. It becomes a significant cost where it applies across a substantial portion of the temp book for several months into the new financial year.

The EOFY review that matters most for a recruitment business is a contract-by-contract examination of which rate agreements accommodate the July change, which require a formal notice process, and which create a period of confirmed margin compression that needs to be quantified and planned for before the year turns.

The Permanent Placement Timing Issue at EOFY

Permanent placements carry a different financial profile. Revenue is recognised at placement, and most recruiters offer a replacement guarantee covering the first 60 to 90 days if a candidate exits during the probationary period. Placements made in April, May, and June sit inside their guarantee window at 30 June.

At year end, the contingent exposure across the active guarantee book is not always clearly mapped. For businesses with strong permanent placement volume in the second half of the financial year, the outstanding guarantees represent real exposure to replacement work in July and August without additional revenue. Knowing which placements are still inside their guarantee window and which have passed expiry gives the business an honest picture of where the contingent risk sits as the year closes.

The receivables position deserves the same attention. Corporate clients of recruitment businesses commonly operate on 30 to 60-day payment terms, and a busy June placement month creates a corresponding receivables build. Mapping the June billing cycle against expected settlement dates, and comparing that timing to the first July payroll obligations, shows the real opening cash position of the new financial year before it arrives.

Building the July Picture Before June 30

Three pieces of work make the most practical difference in the final days of June.

A contract review across the active temp book identifies which charge rate agreements accommodate the award increase, which require a formal notice process, and which will generate confirmed margin compression in July and August. The output is a clear list of clients who need to be contacted before the financial year closes, in the sequence that meets any contractual notice requirements.

A receivables review maps the June billing cycle against expected settlement dates, so the business understands exactly when June revenue will arrive relative to July payroll obligations. For clients on 45-day terms, June invoices settle in mid-August. The first July payroll runs in the opening week of the month. The gap between those two timing points is a genuine working capital consideration that rarely appears in planning conversations but consistently appears in July bank statements.

A Cost and Margin Deep Dive across the temp book, using the full year of EOFY data, identifies which client relationships and role classifications are generating margin at or above the target threshold and which have drifted below. Applying the July award increase to each contract shows which arrangements remain commercially viable and which need a pricing conversation before the new year moves further from the last review date. For businesses where cash flow visibility has been reactive rather than structured, this analysis is often the first time the margin position by client has been seen clearly.

ProfitPulse works with recruitment and labour hire businesses across Queensland and NSW at this point in the calendar. If the July rate review has not been completed and the forward cash position has not been mapped against the new award costs, both are worth building before the financial year closes. Book a discovery call to start that work.

faqs.q: How does the annual award wage increase affect a recruitment or labour hire business margin?
faqs.a: The award increase flows directly into the on-cost stack for every temp worker on a modern award. The base rate rises, and superannuation, some shift allowances, and leave accruals rise proportionally because they are calculated on ordinary time earnings. The aggregate impact on total hourly cost is consistently larger than the headline percentage alone suggests. Where client charge rates do not move at the same time, the margin per hour compresses from the first full pay period of July.

faqs.q: What should a recruitment business do before the end of financial year in Australia?
faqs.a: Three reviews are most useful. First, examine every active temp book contract to identify which charge rate agreements accommodate the July award increase automatically, which require formal written notice before a rate change can apply, and which will create confirmed margin compression in the new financial year. Second, map June billing against expected settlement dates to understand the July cash position before it arrives. Third, review the permanent placement guarantee book to quantify the contingent replacement exposure outstanding at 30 June. A Cost and Margin Deep Dive using the year's EOFY data supports all three.

faqs.q: How do I calculate the full on-cost of a temp worker in Queensland?
faqs.a: Start with the applicable award base rate for the role classification, then add superannuation at the current guarantee rate, annual leave loading where the award provides it, payroll tax at the Queensland threshold and applicable rate, workers compensation premiums for the relevant occupation class, and any penalty rates or shift allowances applicable to the roster. The total on-cost per hour is materially above the base award rate in most placement scenarios. Comparing this total to your current charge rate reveals the actual margin, which may differ significantly from the assumed margin if on-costs have not been modelled recently.

faqs.q: What is a temp placement margin and how is it affected by award rate changes?
faqs.a: Temp margin is the difference between the charge rate invoiced to the client and the full on-cost of employing the worker for that period, including base rate, superannuation, leave accruals, payroll tax, and workers compensation. Because several elements of the on-cost stack are expressed as a percentage of the base rate, the dollar margin per hour moves whenever award rates change. Charge rates do not adjust automatically unless the agreement with the client specifically provides for it, which is why a contract review before 1 July matters.

faqs.q: How does payroll tax affect temp placement margins for recruitment businesses in Queensland?
faqs.a: Payroll tax applies to wages above the Queensland threshold. For a recruitment business acting as employer of record for on-hire workers, payroll tax is calculated on the total wages paid to those workers where the aggregate Queensland payroll exceeds the threshold. This cost forms part of the on-cost stack that determines the real margin per placement. Businesses that grow their on-hire headcount through a financial year without recalculating their payroll tax position sometimes discover the liability at EOFY rather than managing it progressively. A Queensland-based fractional CFO typically includes this monitoring as a standard part of the monthly financial review.

faqs.q: What valuation multiple does an Australian recruitment or labour hire business attract?
faqs.a: Recruitment businesses typically attract three to five times normalised annual profit. Temp-heavy books with diversified client lists and low revenue concentration sit toward the lower end; businesses with recurring contract relationships and predictable perm revenue can attract higher multiples. The key valuation drivers are client concentration, the mix of temp versus perm revenue, and whether the placement network is transferable or personally held by the founder. A business valuation establishes the current baseline and identifies which structural factors are most compressing the multiple.

faqs.q: How does a fractional CFO help a recruitment or labour hire business with financial planning?
faqs.a: Recruitment businesses manage payroll-intensive operations with thin margins and lumpy placement revenue that can be difficult to forecast. A fractional CFO arrangement typically brings temp margin tracking by client, receivables management discipline, and forward cash modelling that keeps the business from being surprised by the consequences of award changes, seasonal volume swings, or debtor concentration. For businesses between two and fifteen million in revenue, it provides financial oversight proportionate to the business's complexity without the cost of a full-time CFO hire.
